---
title: "Housing"
description: "House prices, affordability, rent, and household debt — Canada and OECD peers"
---

How much it costs to rent or buy a home is a defining question for most Canadians.
The charts below track house prices, the gap between home prices and incomes, rent,
and how much debt households carry. Inflation has its own page:
[Cost of Living](../economics/cost-of-living.qmd).

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import (
    CANADA_COLOR, CATEGORICAL_COLORS, PEER_COLOR, HIGHLIGHT_WIDTH, PEER_WIDTH,
    HIGHLIGHT_COUNTRY, PEER_COUNTRIES, get_data_date,
)
from pipeline.charts import peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar, single_line, category_bar
```

## Where Canada stands

A scorecard of where Canada ranks among the 17 peers on each measure (latest available year). Dots are ordered by rank, so **further right is more favourable for Canada** (lower-is-better measures are flipped accordingly). Canada is red, key comparators in colour; hover a dot for its value and rank.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import page_snapshot
page_snapshot("housing").show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Real house prices

Inflation-adjusted ("real") house price index, set to 100 in 2015. Because it
removes general inflation, it shows how house prices have moved relative to the
overall cost of living. Canada is shown in red against OECD peers.

```{python}
#| echo: false
path = "data/housing/oecd_house_price_real.csv"
if os.path.exists(path):
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "house_price_real",
        title="", yaxis_title="Real house price index (2015 = 100)",
        show_average=True, initial_start=2014)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Real house price data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Real house price growth since 2015: ranked

Each country's index equals 100 in 2015, so this ranks **how much real house prices have risen since 2015** — not which country's housing is most expensive today. A rebased index can't show price *levels*; it shows change from a common starting point.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(path):
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "house_price_real", "Real house prices vs 2015 (index, 2015 = 100)",
        f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f} (2015=100)<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## House prices relative to incomes

The price-to-income ratio divides house prices by household disposable income
(indexed to 2015 = 100). It is a standard affordability gauge: when the line
rises, homes are becoming more expensive relative to what households earn.

```{python}
#| echo: false
path2 = "data/housing/oecd_house_price_income.csv"
if os.path.exists(path2):
    df = pd.read_csv(path2)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "price_to_income",
        title="", yaxis_title="Price-to-income ratio (2015 = 100)",
        show_average=True, initial_start=2014)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path2)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Price-to-income data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Change in price-to-income since 2015: ranked

This series is also rebased to 2015 = 100, so it ranks the **change** in the price-to-income ratio since 2015 — i.e. how much affordability has shifted — not the level of the ratio across countries.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(path2):
    df = pd.read_csv(path2)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "price_to_income", "Price-to-income vs 2015 (index, 2015 = 100)",
        f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path2)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f} (2015=100)<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## New housing prices in Canada

Statistics Canada's New Housing Price Index tracks changes in builders' selling
prices of new homes (the "total" of house and land), monthly. This is a
Canada-specific series shown over time. It covers builder-sold new houses, so it
does not capture condominium apartments, whose prices can move differently.

```{python}
#| echo: false
nhpi_path = "data/housing/statcan_nhpi.csv"
if os.path.exists(nhpi_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(nhpi_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "nhpi", title="",
                      yaxis_title="New Housing Price Index (Dec 2016 = 100)",
                      rangeslider=True,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0205-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(nhpi_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("New Housing Price Index not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Home prices vs. incomes

House prices and household incomes, both in **real (inflation-adjusted) terms** and indexed to 2000 = 100 so they share one scale. Plotting the two paths together shows the affordability gap directly: the wider they spread, the more home prices have outrun what households actually earn. The price line is the OECD's real house-price index for Canada (which reflects the resale market); the income line is Statistics Canada's median after-tax income, also in constant dollars. (The New Housing Price Index isn't used here — it tracks builders' prices for *new* homes, which have lagged the resale run-up.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
rprice_path = "data/housing/oecd_house_price_real.csv"
inc_path = "data/income/statcan_median_income.csv"
if os.path.exists(rprice_path) and os.path.exists(inc_path):
    BASE = 2000
    rp = pd.read_csv(rprice_path)
    rp = rp[rp["country_code"] == "CAN"].sort_values("year")
    rp["idx"] = rp["house_price_real"] / rp.loc[rp["year"] == BASE, "house_price_real"].iloc[0] * 100

    inc = pd.read_csv(inc_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    inc["year"] = inc["date"].dt.year
    inc["idx"] = inc["median_income"] / inc.loc[inc["year"] == BASE, "median_income"].iloc[0] * 100
    inc = inc[inc["year"] >= BASE]

    fig = go.Figure()
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=rp["year"], y=rp["idx"], name="House prices (real)",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=3),
        hovertemplate="House prices: %{y:.0f}<extra>%{x}</extra>"))
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=inc["year"], y=inc["idx"], name="Median after-tax income (real)",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color="#1f77b4", width=3),
        hovertemplate="Income: %{y:.0f}<extra>%{x}</extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white",
        yaxis=dict(title=f"Index ({BASE} = 100, real)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5"),
                   range=[1999, int(rp["year"].max())]),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, xanchor="left", x=0),
        margin=dict(b=90, t=50),
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Sources: OECD real house-price index & Statistics Canada median after-tax income (11-10-0190-01) | Data as of: {get_data_date(inc_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30, xanchor="left",
            showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Price/income data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Actual home prices by city and type

The charts above use index and proxy measures because actual transaction prices are tightly licensed. The figures in this section use the **CREA MLS® Home Price Index** — the real estate industry's benchmark for the price of a "typical" home in each market. Unlike the census's owner-estimated values or the New Housing Price Index, this tracks actual resale prices and breaks them out by dwelling type.

This first chart shows the latest **benchmark price by city and dwelling type** — detached houses, townhouses, and apartments/condos — across Canada's major markets.

```{python}
#| echo: false
crea_ok = False
try:
    from pipeline.crea import (load_crea, fig_price_by_type, fig_price_time_series,
                               fig_price_to_income)
    _sheets, _agg, _month = load_crea(".")
    crea_ok = True
    fig_price_by_type(_sheets, _month).show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except Exception as e:
    print("CREA price data temporarily unavailable.")
```

## House prices over time, by city and type

How the benchmark price of a typical home has moved in each major market, by dwelling type — detached, townhouse, or apartment/condo. This is the run-up that the index and proxy charts above can only hint at.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if crea_ok:
    fig_price_time_series(_sheets).show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("CREA price data temporarily unavailable.")
```

## How many years of income a home costs

Dividing the national benchmark price by median after-tax income gives an intuitive affordability gauge: how many years of a typical household's after-tax income it would take to buy the typical home. (The benchmark is deflated to 2024 dollars via CPI so it is comparable with the inflation-adjusted income series.) The ratio has climbed from roughly **5.7× in 2005 to 9.3× in 2024**. Use the selector to switch from the typical home to a specific dwelling type — a detached house now costs more than **10× income**, while an apartment or condo stays under **7×**.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if crea_ok:
    fig_price_to_income(_agg, ".").show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("CREA price data temporarily unavailable.")
```

## Home values by city

Sale prices by city aren't openly available, but the census asks owners to estimate their home's value, which gives a good general guide. This maps **median dwelling value** across metropolitan areas (2021 Census). **Zoom and pan to your area.** Owner-estimated, 2021; city-level (CMA/CA).

For the full **neighbourhood-level** view — every census tract, where you can zoom right to your own street — open the dedicated map (kept on its own page so this one stays fast to load):

**[→ Home value by neighbourhood (census-tract map)](neighbourhoods.qmd)**

The same census tracts also carry [median household income](../income/neighbourhoods.qmd)
and [median age](../population/age-neighbourhoods.qmd) maps — flip between them to see
how prices, incomes, and who lives where (young families at the urban edge, retirees in
long-settled areas) line up across a city.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_map
cma_geo_path = "data/geo/cma_2021.geojson"
cma_path = "data/geo/statcan_cma_indicators.csv"
if os.path.exists(cma_geo_path) and os.path.exists(cma_path):
    cma_geo = json.load(open(cma_geo_path))
    cma = pd.read_csv(cma_path, dtype={"cmauid": str})
    sub = cma.dropna(subset=["dwelling_value"])
    fig = choropleth_map(cma_geo, sub, "cmauid", "dwelling_value", name_col="name",
        colorbar_title="Median home<br>value", value_prefix="$", value_fmt=",.0f", colorscale="Viridis",
        zmin=float(sub["dwelling_value"].quantile(0.05)), zmax=float(sub["dwelling_value"].quantile(0.95)),
        source_note="Source: Statistics Canada, 2021 Census; owner-estimated dwelling value.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Home-value map not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## Affordability by city (home value ÷ income)

Home value divided by median household income — roughly how many years of a typical household's income a typical home costs. It turns the price map into an *affordability* map: the same $1M home is far less attainable where incomes are lower. **Zoom and pan to your area.** (2021 Census; owner-estimated value ÷ median household income.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(cma_geo_path) and os.path.exists(cma_path):
    sub = cma.dropna(subset=["value_to_income"])
    fig = choropleth_map(cma_geo, sub, "cmauid", "value_to_income", name_col="name",
        colorbar_title="Home value<br>÷ income", value_suffix="×", value_fmt=".1f", colorscale="YlOrRd",
        zmin=float(sub["value_to_income"].quantile(0.05)), zmax=float(sub["value_to_income"].quantile(0.95)),
        source_note="Source: Statistics Canada, 2021 Census; owner-estimated dwelling value ÷ median household income.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Affordability map not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## Rent in Canada

The Consumer Price Index for rent — what renters pay over time. Renters are roughly a third of Canadian households, so rent is a central part of the cost-of-living picture that owner-focused house-price indices miss.

```{python}
#| echo: false
rent_path = "data/housing/statcan_rent_cpi.csv"
if os.path.exists(rent_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(rent_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "rent_index", title="",
                      yaxis_title="Rent CPI (2002 = 100)",
                      rangeslider=True, initial_start=2014,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0004-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(rent_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Rent data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Housing starts

The number of new homes on which construction began, monthly. Starts are the clearest gauge of new housing *supply* — the other side of the affordability story from the prices, rents, and demand above. Whether new supply keeps pace with population growth is central to the debate.

```{python}
#| echo: false
starts_path = "data/housing/statcan_housing_starts.csv"
if os.path.exists(starts_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(starts_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "housing_starts", title="",
                      yaxis_title="Housing starts (units, monthly)", rangeslider=True,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada / CMHC, Table 34-10-0143-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(starts_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Housing starts data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Rental vacancy rate

The share of purpose-built rental apartments sitting empty, across census metropolitan areas. A low vacancy rate signals a tight rental market with little slack — upward pressure on rents and harder searches for tenants.

```{python}
#| echo: false
vac_path = "data/housing/statcan_vacancy_rate.csv"
if os.path.exists(vac_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(vac_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "vacancy_rate", title="",
                      yaxis_title="Vacancy rate (%)", rangeslider=True,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada / CMHC, Table 34-10-0127-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(vac_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Vacancy rate data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

The national average hides wide variation between cities. In the latest survey, vacancy ranged from under 2% in the tightest markets — where rentals are hardest to find and rents rise fastest — to over 6% in the loosest. Canada's largest metros are highlighted.

```{python}
#| echo: false
cmavac_path = "data/housing/statcan_cma_vacancy.csv"
if os.path.exists(cmavac_path):
    cv = pd.read_csv(cmavac_path)
    yr = int(cv["year"].iloc[0])
    fig = category_bar(cv, "cma", "vacancy_rate", xaxis_title="Vacancy rate (%)",
        value_fmt=".1f", ticksuffix="%",
        highlight={"Toronto", "Vancouver", "Montréal", "Calgary", "Ottawa-Gatineau", "Edmonton"},
        highlight_color="#f28e2b",
        hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:.1f}%<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada / CMHC, Table 34-10-0127-01 — {yr}.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("CMA vacancy data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Household debt

Household debt as a percentage of net disposable income. A value of 180% means
households owe $1.80 for every dollar of annual after-tax income. Most of this debt
is mortgages, with consumer credit and other loans making up the rest. It captures how
leveraged households are — relevant to both affordability and financial risk.

```{python}
#| echo: false
debt_path = "data/housing/oecd_household_debt.csv"
if os.path.exists(debt_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(debt_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "household_debt",
        title="", yaxis_title="% of net disposable income",
        show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Household Dashboard | Data as of: {get_data_date(debt_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Household debt data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest household debt: ranked

**Data current to `{python} get_data_date("data/housing/oecd_household_debt.csv")`.**

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(debt_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(debt_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "household_debt", "% of net disposable income",
        f"Source: OECD Household Dashboard | Data as of: {get_data_date(debt_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f}%<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Household debt payments

The debt level above measures how much households *owe*; the **debt service ratio** measures how much they *pay* — the share of disposable income that goes to obligated debt payments (interest **and** principal) each quarter. It is the more direct gauge of financial stress, because it rises when interest rates climb even if the amount owed stays flat. The ratio fell sharply in 2020, when policy-rate cuts and mortgage-payment deferrals eased payments, then climbed back to around its historical high as households renewed mortgages at higher rates. Mortgage payments are the larger and more interest-rate-sensitive of the two components.

```{python}
#| echo: false
dsr_path = "data/housing/statcan_debt_service_ratio.csv"
if os.path.exists(dsr_path):
    d = pd.read_csv(dsr_path, parse_dates=["date"]).sort_values("date")
    figd = go.Figure()
    # Stacked area: mortgage + non-mortgage payments sum exactly to the total DSR,
    # so the stack height is the headline ratio and the split shows what drives it.
    # customdata carries the raw (un-stacked) component value for the hover.
    figd.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["date"], y=d["dsr_mortgage"], name="Mortgage",
        mode="lines", line=dict(width=0.5, color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0]), stackgroup="dsr",
        customdata=d["dsr_mortgage"],
        hovertemplate="Mortgage: %{customdata:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    figd.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["date"], y=d["dsr_nonmortgage"], name="Non-mortgage (consumer)",
        mode="lines", line=dict(width=0.5, color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[1]), stackgroup="dsr",
        customdata=d["dsr_nonmortgage"],
        hovertemplate="Non-mortgage: %{customdata:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    figd.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["date"], y=d["dsr_total"], name="Total",
        mode="lines", line=dict(width=2, color="#333333"),
        hovertemplate="Total: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    _xmax = d["date"].max()
    figd.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="% of disposable income", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   ticksuffix="%", rangemode="tozero"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            range=[pd.Timestamp("2006-01-01"), _xmax],
            rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=120, r=20))
    figd.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada 11-10-0065-01 (seasonally adjusted) | Data as of: {get_data_date(dsr_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figd.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Debt service ratio data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download data

- [Real House Price Index (CSV)](../data/housing/oecd_house_price_real.csv) — OECD Analytical House Prices
- [Price-to-Income Ratio (CSV)](../data/housing/oecd_house_price_income.csv) — OECD Analytical House Prices
- [New Housing Price Index (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_nhpi.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 18-10-0205-01
- [Rent, CPI (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_rent_cpi.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 18-10-0004-01
- [Housing Starts (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_housing_starts.csv) — Statistics Canada / CMHC Table 34-10-0143-01
- [Rental Vacancy Rate (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_vacancy_rate.csv) — Statistics Canada / CMHC Table 34-10-0127-01
- [Rental Vacancy Rate by City (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_cma_vacancy.csv) — Statistics Canada / CMHC Table 34-10-0127-01
- [City home value, value-to-income & more (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_cma_indicators.csv) — 2021 Census (owner-estimated dwelling value)
- [Home value by Neighbourhood / census tract (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_ct_dwelling_value_2021.csv) — 2021 Census (owner-estimated dwelling value)
- [Household Debt (CSV)](../data/housing/oecd_household_debt.csv) — OECD Household Dashboard
- [Household Debt Service Ratio (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_debt_service_ratio.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 11-10-0065-01

Mortgage and bond-yield data are on [Borrowing Costs](../economics/borrowing-costs.qmd).
